canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, multi-agent debate is consistently described as an interactive, multi-turn process involving multiple agents collaborating to refine an outcome. The terms 'multi-round discussion' and 'multi-turn debate' are also used to refer to this behavior. The common goal is for agents to exchange information, reasoning steps, or critiques to reach a consensus, refine conclusions, or improve system designs. One definition specifies that agents may begin with "differing initial viewpoints" and engage in "reflective discourse, critically revising their stances through mutual exchange" (TS-CLIP: Time Series Understanding byCLIP). As a research task, this behavior is operationalized in the context of logical reasoning. For example, one paper notes the practice of building "multi-agent debate tasks on several mathematical or logical reasoning datasets" (PRISM: Efficient Long-Range Reasoning With Short-ContextLLMs). Multi-agent debate is also studied in relation to self-consistency.
expert_removed_reason: method/engineering — not a construct/measurement/behavior of LLM ability

MentalGLMSeries: Explainable Large Language Models for Mental Health Analysis onChinese Social Media
PRISM: Efficient Long-Range Reasoning With Short-ContextLLMs

definitions.text: A collaborative process where multiple agents with differing initial viewpoints discuss and revise their positions to reach consensus on the best response.
An interactive process where multiple agents engage in several rounds of dialogue to exchange information and refine their conclusions.
definitions.paper: MentalGLMSeries: Explainable Large Language Models for Mental Health Analysis onChinese Social Media

definitions.text: An interactive behavior where multiple LLM agents exchange reasoning steps or critiques over several turns to refine outputs or system designs.
definitions.paper: Explaining Differences Between Model Pairs in Natural Language through Sample Learning

relationships.evidence: Inspired by Du et al., we build multi-agent debate tasks on several mathematical or logical reasoning datasets. (Section 4.2.1)
